## AE

1. The Adverse Events dataset includes clinical data describing "any untoward medical occurrence in a patient or clinical.
2. AE description and coding
   a. AETERM captures the verbatim term collected for the event.
   b. AEMODIFY Perm and should be included if the sponsor's procedure permits modification of a.
   c. AEDECOD = dictionary preferred term.
   d. AEBODSYS is the system organ class (SOC) from the coding dictionary associated with the.
3. Additional categorization and grouping
   a. AECAT and AESCAT should not be redundant with the domain code or dictionary classification.
   b. AEGRPID may be used to link (or associate) different records together to form a block of.
4. Prespecified terms; presence or absence of events
   a. An AEPRESP value of "Y" is used to indicate that the event in AETERM was prespecified on the.
   b. If it is important to know which adverse events from a prespecified list were not reported as.
   c. If a study collects both prespecified adverse events and free-text events.
   d. Records should be included in the submission AE dataset only for adverse events that have.
5. Timing variables
   a. AEENRF may be used when this relative timing assessment is made coincident with the end of the.
   b. Additional timing variables (e.g., AEDTC) may be used when appropriate.
6. Actions taken
   a. AECONTRT is a Y/N variable.
   b. Actions other than concomitant treatments are recorded in: - AEACN.
7. Other qualifier variables
   a. If categories of serious events are collected secondarily to a leading question the values of.
   b. The serious categories "Involves cancer" (AESCAN) and "Occurred with overdose" (AESOD) are not.
   c. When a description of "Other Medically Important Serious Adverse Events" category is collected.
   d. In studies using toxicity grade according to a standard toxicity scale such as the CTCAE v3.0.
   e. It is the sponsor's responsibility to define an event.
   i. One record per adverse event per subject for each unique event.
      ii. One record per adverse event per subject.
      iii. The domain-level metadata (see § 3.2, Using the CDISC Domain Models in Regulatory Submissions.
8. Use of EPOCH and TAETORD: When EPOCH is included in the AE domain, it should be the epoch of the start of the adverse event.
9. Any additional identifier variables may be added to the AE domain.
10. The following qualifiers would not be used in AE: --OCCUR, --STAT, and--REASND.
11. Variable order in the domain should follow the rules see § 4.1.4, Order of the Variables, and the order § 1.1, Purpose.
12. The addition of AELLT, AELLTCD, AEPTCD, AEHLT, AEHLTCD, AEHLGT, AEHLGTCD, AEBDSYCD, AESOC.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/AG/assumptions.md -->
## AG

1. Purpose of the domain: Some tests involve administration of substances.
   a. The Concomitant/Prior Medications (CM) domain seemed particularly inappropriate when the.
   b. The AG domain was created to fill this gap.
   c. The AG domain has advantages over the Procedures (PR) domain for this purpose.
   d. Information about the conduct of the procedure with which the procedure agent administration.
2. Examples and structure
   a. Examples of agents administered as part of a procedure include a short-acting bronchodilator.
   b. The structure of the AG domain is 1 record per agent intervention episode.
3. AG description and coding
   a. AGTRT captures the name of the agent and it is topic var.
   b. AGMODIFY should be included if the sponsor's procedure permits modification of a verbatim term.
   c. AGDECOD is the standardized agent term derived by the sponsor from the coding dictionary.
4. Prespecified terms; presence or absence of procedure agents
   a. AGPRESP is used to indicate whether an agent was prespecified.
   b. AGOCCUR is used to indicate whether a prespecified agent was used.
   c. If an agent was not prespecified, the value of AGOCCUR should be null.
5. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or interventions general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the AG domain.
   b. The variables --DOSTOT and --DOSRGM, although allowed.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/BE/assumptions.md -->
## BE

**BE - Specification**
be.xpt, Biospecimen Events - Events.
**BE - Assumptions**
1. The BE domain contains data about actions taken that affect or may affect a specimen, such as specimen collection.
2. The value in BEREFID identifies the specimen most affected by the event.
3. BELOC holds the relevant anatomic location of the subject, so it should only be populated when the subject participates in and is.
4. BEPARTY and BEPRTYID together identify the individual or organization that takes responsibility for the biospecimen as a result.
5. Usually BEPARTY and BEPRTYID refer to who has possession of the biospecimen after the action in BETERM.
6. Timing variables:
   a. BESTDTC and BEENDTC hold the start and end date/times for the event given in BETERM.
   b. Unlike other Events domains, BEDTC does not hold the date/time of data collection.
   c. VISITNUM, VISIT, and VISITDY values for all records refer to the visit in which the originally.
7. The following variables generally would not be used in BE: dictionary coding variables , AE-specific variables.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/BS/assumptions.md -->
## BS

1. BS is not restricted to PGx-related specimens.
2. For biospecimens of genetic material, BSSPEC values are drawn from the GENSMP (C111114) codelist.
3. Non-genetic BSSPEC values are drawn from the SPEC (C77529) codelist, which is part of the SEND terminology listing.
4. To adapt BS for use with the SDTMIG, use the SPECTYPE (C78734) codelist in BSSPEC, add --LOC, --LAT, --DIR.
5. The following variables generally would not be used in BS: --POS, --ORNLO, --ORNHI, --STNRLO, --STNRHI, --STNRC, --NRIND, --LEAD.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/CE/assumptions.md -->
## CE

1. The determination of events to be considered clinical events versus adverse events should be done carefully and with reference to.
   a. Events considered to be clinical events may include episodes of symptoms of the disease under.
   b. If any event is considered serious, then the serious variable (--SER) and the serious adverse.
   c. Other studies might track the occurrence of specific events as efficacy endpoints.
2. CEOCCUR and CEPRESP are used together to indicate whether the event in CETERM was prespecified and whether it occurred.
3. The collection of write-in events on a CE CRF should be considered with caution.
4. Any identifier variable may be added to the CE domain.
5. Timing variables
   a. CESTRF or CEENRF may be used when this timing assessment is relative to the study reference.
   b. Additional timing variables may be used when appropriate.
6. The clinical events domain is based on the Events general observation class and thus can use any variables in the Events class.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/CM/assumptions.md -->
## CM

**CM - Description/Overview**
An interventions domain that contains concomitant and prior medications used by the subject.
**CM - Specification**
cm.xpt, Concomitant/Prior Medications - Interventions.
**CM - Assumptions**
1. The structure of the CM domain is 1 record per medication intervention episode, constant-dosing interval.
2. CM description and coding
   a. CMTRT is topic var and captures the name of the concomitant medication/therapy or the.
   b. CMMODIFY should be included if the sponsor's procedure permits modification of a verbatim term.
   c. CMDECOD is the standardized medication/therapy term derived by the sponsor from the coding.
   d. When CMDECOD values from the WHODrug Dictionary are longer than 200 characters.
3. Prespecified terms; presence or absence of concomitant medications
   a. CMPRESP and CMOCCUR are used together to indicate the intervention in CMTRT was prespecified.
   b. CMOCCUR is used to indicate whether a prespecified medication was used.
   c. If a medication was not prespecified, the value of CMOCCUR should be null.
4. Variables for timing relative to a time point
   a. CMSTRTPT, CMSTTPT, CMENRTPT, and CMENTPT may be populated as necessary to indicate when a.
5. Although any identifier, timing variables, or interventions general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the CM domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/CO/assumptions.md -->
## CO

**§5 Models for Special-purpose Domains**
Special-purpose Domains is an SDTM class in its own right.
**5.1 Comments (CO)**
**CO - Description/Overview**
A special-purpose domain that contains comments that may be collected alongside other data.
**CO - Specification**
co.xpt, Comments - Special Purpose.
¹In this column, an asterisk (*) indicates that the var may be CT.
**CO - Assumptions**
1. The Comments special-purpose domain provides a solution for submitting free-text comments related to data in 1 or more SDTM.
2. Although the structure for the Comments domain in the SDTM is "One record per comment".
3. The CO dataset accommodates 3 sources of comments:
   a. Those unrelated to a specific domain or parent record(s).
   b. Those related to a domain but not to specific parent record(s).
   c. Those related to a specific parent record or group of parent records.
4. When the comment text is longer than 200 characters, the first 200 characters of the comment will be in COVAL.
5. The variable COREF may be null unless it is used to identify the source of the comment.
6. Identifier variables and Timing variables may be added to the CO domain.

## CP

**CP - Description/Overview**
A findings domain that contains data related to the characterization of cell phenotype, lineage.
The CP domain is modeled for use with disseminated tissue specimens and cell suspensions.
The domain is intended to support tests associated with a cell phenotyping component based on the use of markers and is not.
The CP domain is not intended to supplant use of the LB domain for routine lab hematology (e.g., blood cell differentials).
The modeled use cases include measurement of
- cell populations identified, classified, and/or otherwise characterized based on the differential expression of phenotypic.
To provide the flexibility needed to report cell marker expression data, which can range widely in complexity.
Most of the new variables are permissible, and are available as needed to fully define a test and/or to prevent ambiguity that.
New variables include --SBMRKS (Sublineage Marker String), --CELSTA (Cell State), --CSMRKS (Cell State Marker String).
Definitions and appropriate use of these variables are provided in the Specification and Assumptions sections of this guidance.
Data submitters should work closely with laboratory data providers, analysts.
Sponsors that previously chose to submit cell phenotyping data in the LB domain.
Several of the new variables (i.e., --SBMRKS, --CELSTA, --CSMRKS) are used to further subdivide the population reported in --TEST.
This approach provides a more easily understood test name in the --TEST variable and enables development of CT for --TEST and.
The goal is to standardize, where possible, cell phenotype test names across studies so that it will be easier for users to.
This approach also enhances the ability to integrate and compare data across studies in a practicable manner that (in addition to.
Used in accordance with this guidance, the complete marker sting information provided in the --MRKSTR variable reflects the.
Together with the gating information provided in the --GATE and --GATEDEF variables.
To facilitate these objectives and to enable accurate cross-study comparisons and data-mining efforts.
**CP - Assumptions**
1. The Cell Phenotype domain captures cell phenotyping and related data based on cell expression markers and other indicators (e.g.
2. The CP domain is only used for tests which include a phenotyping component that relies on using cell markers to identify a.
3. A value which is calculated and reported by a lab according to its procedures is considered collected rather than derived; the.
4. CPCELSTA is used in conjunction with CPCSMRKS.
5. The combination of values in CPTEST, CPSBMRKS, CPCELSTA, and CPCSMRKS are used to uniquely identify a test.
6. Populating the CPTEST and CPMRKSTR variables: The general structure of CPTEST and CPMRKSTR depends on the use case.
   a. Immunophenotyping
   i. CPTEST is populated with the name of the cell type being measured.
      ii. expected: CPMRKSTR is populated, and that it contains the entire set of markers used to define.
      iii. Marker strings follow, as closely as possible, formatting recommendations presented in.
   b. Quantitative single-marker expression
   i. CPTEST begins with the identity of the marker (e.g., CD99).
      ii. expected: CPMRKSTR is populated, and that it starts by identifying the marker being quantified.
      iii. It is recommended that the delimiting text is the abbreviation for the unit of measure used to.
      iv. Marker strings follow, as closely as possible, formatting recommendations presented in.
   c. In the case of target/receptor occupancy a more generalized test value is populated into.
7. Specifying viability:
   a. Because the majority of cell phenotyping tests of interest are for viable cells.
   b. Viability marker(s) used to define a test are included in the full marker string in CPMRKSTR.
8. Recommended formatting of marker string variables CPMRKSTR, CPSBMRKS.
   a. Marker strings do not contain delimiting characters (e.g., ",", space, "/".
   b. Forward slash "/" is only used to separate the portion of the marker string defining a.
   c. When referring to a marker using the cluster of differentiation (CD) designation.
   d. This order maintains alignment with how a test is identified using the ordered combination of.
   e. Forward (FSC) and Side (SSC) light scatter: These parameters are generally used to perform.
   f. Indicating the expression level of individual markers included in a marker string: A variety.
   i. neg, min, or - to denote the absence or minimal expression (e.g., CD4neg, CD4min, CD4-)
      ii. pos or + to denote that the marker is expressed (e.g., CD4pos, CD4+)
      iii. high, hi, or ++ to denote that the marker is expressed at a very high level relative to simply.
      iv. other formats
   g. Five designations are recommended for use in SDTM datasets:
   i. "-"
      ii. "lo" (the marker is expressed at a low level)
      iii. "mid"
      iv. "+"
   v. "hi"
      vi. The data provider must determine the best way to designate an expression level suited to the.
   h. An example of a marker often associated with a need to indicate cell location is CD152.
9. CPNRIND can be added to indicate where a result falls with respect to a reference range defined by CPORNRLO and CPORNRHI (e.g.
10. The variable CPORRESU uses the UNIT codelist.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/CV/assumptions.md -->
## CV

1. The Cardiovascular System Findings domain is used to represent results and findings of cardiovascular diagnostic procedures.
2. Any Identifiers, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the CV domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DA/assumptions.md -->
## DA

1. This domain records the amount of study product transferred to or from the study subject.
   a. See the SDTMIG for Medical Devices (available at cdisc.org).
   b. For drugs, transfers are usually recorded using the tests "Dispensed Amount" and "Returned.
   c. Test terminology for other products may be different; for example, for nutrition.
2. DACAT may be used to differentiate transfers of different groups of products.
3. DAREFID and DASPID are both available for capturing label information.
4. The following qualifiers would not generally be used in DA: --MODIFY, --POS, --BODSYS, --ORNRLO, --ORNRHI, --STNRLO, --STNRHI.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DD/assumptions.md -->
## DD

1. There may be more than 1 cause of death.
2. Death details are typically collected on designated CRF pages.
3. Autopsy information should be handled as per recommendations in the Procedures (PR) domain.
4. There are separate codelists for DD tests and responses.
5. Any identifiers, timing variables, or findings general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the DD domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DI/assumptions.md -->
## DI

**DI - Description/Overview (§9.1)**
The DI dataset was introduced as part of the SDTMIG for Medical Devices (SDTMIG-MD).

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DM/assumptions.md -->
## DM

**DM - Description/Overview**
A special-purpose domain that includes a set of essential standard variables that describe each subject in a clinical study.
It is the parent domain for all other observations for human clinical subjects.
**DM - Assumptions**
1. CDISC assumes that SITEID will always be present, with INVID and INVNAM used as necessary.
2. Every subject in a study must have a subject identifier (SUBJID).
3. Concerns for subject privacy suggest caution regarding the collection of variables like BRTHDTC.
4. With the exception of trials that use multistage processes to assign subjects to arms as described below.
   a. Rules for the arm-related variables:
   i. If ARMCD is null, then ARM must be null and ARMNRS populated (Req) with the reason ARMCD is.
      ii. If ACTARMCD is null, then ACTARM must be null and ARMNRS populated (Req) with the reason.
      iii. ARMNRS may not be populated if both ARMCD and ACTARMCD are populated.
      iv. If ARMNRS is populated with "UNPLANNED TREATMENT".
   b. Multistage assignment to treatment: Some trials use a multistage process for assigning a.
   c. Examples illustrating the arm-related variables
   i. Example 2 below shows how subjects who started the trial but were never assigned to an arm.
      ii. The Subject Elements (SE) dataset records the series of elements a subject passed through in.
5. Study population flags should not be included in SDTM data.
6. Submission of multiple race responses should be represented in the Demographics (DM) domain and Supplemental Qualifiers (SUPPDM).
   a. The Racec-Ethnicc Codetable (available at cdisc.org) represents associations between collected.
7. RFSTDTC, RFENDTC, RFXSTDTC, RFXENDTC, RFCSTDTC, RFCENDTC, RFICDTC, RFPENDTC, DTHDTC, and BRTHDTC represent date/time values.
8. Additional permissible identifier, qualifier, and timing variables:
   a. Only the following timing variables are permissible and may be added as appropriate: VISITNUM.
   b. The order of these additional variables within the domain should follow the rules see § 4.1.4.
9. As § 4.1.4, Order of the Variables, RFSTDTC is used to calculate study day variables.
10. The DM domain contains several pairs of reference period variables: RFSTDTC and RFENDTC, RFXSTDTC and RFXENDTC.
   a. RFSTDTC and RFENDTC: This pair of variables is sponsor-defined.
   b. RFXSTDTC and RFXENDTC: This pair of variables defines a consistent reference period for all.
   c. RFCSTDTC and RFCENDTC: This pair of variables is used only when the study uses a.
   d. RFICDTC and RFPENDTC: The definitions of this pair of variables are consistent in every study.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DS/assumptions.md -->
## DS

1. See ICH E3, §10.1, for information about disposition events.
2. Categorization
   a. DSCAT is used to distinguish between disposition events, protocol milestones, and other events.
   b. An event with DSCAT = "DISPOSITION EVENT" describes either disposition of study participation.
   i. DSSCAT = "STUDY PARTICIPATION" is used to represent disposition of study participation.
      ii. DSSCAT = "STUDY TREATMENT" is used when a study has only a single treatment.
      iii. If a study has multiple treatments, then DSSCAT should name the individual treatment.
   c. DSSCAT may be used when DSCAT = "PROTOCOL MILESTONE" or "OTHER EVENT".
   d. An event with DSCAT = "PROTOCOL MILESTONE" is a protocol-specified, point-in-time event.
   e. An event with DSCAT = "OTHER EVENT" is another important event that occurred during a trial.
   f. Associations between DSCAT and some DSDECOD codelist values are described in the DS Codetable.
3. DS description and coding
   a. DSDECOD values are drawn from CT.
   b. When DSCAT = "DISPOSITION EVENT" DSTERM contains either "COMPLETED" or.
   i. When DSTERM = "COMPLETED", DSDECOD is the term "COMPLETED" from the CT codelist NCOMPLT.
      ii. When DSTERM contains verbatim text, DSDECOD will use the extensible CT Codelist NCOMPLT.
   c. When DSCAT = "PROTOCOL MILESTONE", DSTERM contains the verbatim (as collected) and/or.
   d. When DSCAT = "OTHER EVENT", DSTERM and DSDECOD uses sponsor terminology.
   i. If a reason for the event was collected, the reason for the event is in DSTERM and the DSDECOD.
      ii. If no reason was collected, then DSTERM populated with the value in DSDECOD.
4. Timing variables
   a. DSSTDTC is expected and is used for the date/time of the disposition event.
   b. DSSTDTC documents the date/time that a protocol milestone, disposition event.
   c. EPOCH may be included as a timing variable as in other general observation-class domains.
5. Reasons for termination: ICH E3 §10.1 indicates that "the specific reason for discontinuation" should be presented.
6. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or Events general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the DS domain.
7. For the ABC arm, if drug C is dropped, the subject may transition to an AB element or follow some other defined protocol.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/DV/assumptions.md -->
## DV

1. Events typically include what the event was, captured in --TERM (the topic variable).
2. This domain should not be used to collect entry-criteria information.
3. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or Events general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the DV domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/EC/assumptions.md -->
## EC

1. The EC domain model reflects protocol-specified study treatment administrations, as collected.
   a. EC should be used in all cases where collected exposure information cannot or should not be.
   b. Collected exposure data are in most cases represented in a combination of 1 or more of EC, DA.
   c. Collected exposure log data points descriptive of administrations typically reflect amounts at.
2. Treatment description (ECTRT) is sponsor-defined and should reflect how the protocol-specified study treatment is known or.
3. ECMOOD is permissible; when implemented, it populated (Req) for all records.
   a. Values of ECMOOD, to date include:
   i. "SCHEDULED"
      ii. "PERFORMED"
   b. If ECLOC is determined at the time of administration.
   c. Appropriate timing variable(s) populated.
   d. ECOCCUR is generally not applicable for Scheduled records.
   e. An activity may be rescheduled or modified multiple times before being performed.
4. Doses not taken, not given, or missed
   a. The record qualifier --OCCUR, with value of "N".
   b. Qualifiers that indicate dose amount (e.g., ECDOSE.
   c. If a reason why a dose was not given is collected, it is represented in ECREASOC.
5. Timing variables
   a. Timing variables in the EC domain should reflect administrations by the intervals they were.
   b. For administrations considered given at a point in time.
6. When the relationship between EC and EX records can be described in RELREC, then it should be defined.
7. Additional interventions qualifiers
   a. --DOSTOT is under evaluation for potential deprecation and replacement with a mechanism to.
   b. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or findings general observation-class qualifiers.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/EG/assumptions.md -->
## EG

1. EGREFID is intended to store an identifier (e.g., UUID) for the associated ECG tracing.
2. There are separate codelists for tests and results based on regular 10-second ECGs and for tests and results based on Holter.
   a. Associations between some ECG abnormality tests and response codelists are described in the.
3. For non-individual ECG beat data and for aggregate ECG parameter results (e.g., "QT interval", "RR", "PR", "QRS").
4. For individual-beat parameter results, waveform data will not be stored in the warehouse.
5. The method for QT interval correction is specified in the test name by CT: EGTESTCD = "QTCFAG" and EGTEST = "QTcF Interval.
6. EGBEATNO is used to differentiate between beats in beat-to-beat records.
7. EGREPNUM is used to differentiate between multiple repetitions of a test within a given time frame.
8. EGNRIND can be added to indicate where a result falls with respect to reference range defined by EGORNRLO and EGORNRHI.
9. When "QTcF Interval, Aggregate" or "QTcB Interval, Aggregate" is derived by the sponsor.
10. If this domain is used in conjunction with the ECG QT Correction Model Data (QT) domain:
   a. For each QT correction method used in the study.
   b. The sponsor should assign values for EGTESTCD/EGTEST appropriately with clear documentation on.
11. Any identifiers, timing variables, or findings general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the EG domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/EX/assumptions.md -->
## EX

1. EX structure and use
   a. Treatments that are not protocol-specified should be represented in the Concomitant/Prior.
   b. The EX domain is recognized in most cases as a derived dataset where EXDOSU reflects the.
   c. The EX domain is required for all studies that include protocol-specified study treatment.
   i. Derived from actual observation of the administration of drug by the investigator
      ii. Derived from automated dispensing device that records administrations
      iii. Derived from subject recall
      iv. Derived from product accountability data
   v. Derived from the protocol.
   d. The EX domain should contain 1 record per constant-dosing interval per subject.
   i. a single record per subject, spanning the entire 6-week treatment phase.
      ii. up to 6 records (1 for each weekly administration).
2. Exposure treatment description
   a. EXTRT captures the name of the protocol-specified study treatment and is topic var.
   b. Doses of placebo should be represented by EXTRT = "PLACEBO" and EXDOSE = "0".
3. Categorization and grouping
   a. EXCAT and EXSCAT may be used when appropriate to categorize treatments into categories and.
4. Timing variables
   a. If the subject is only exposed to study medication within a clinical encounter.
   b. For administrations considered given at a point in time.
5. When the relationship between EC and EX records can be described in RELREC, then it should be defined.
6. Additional interventions qualifiers
   a. Because EX includes only treatments received, --MOOD would generally not be used in EX.
   b. --DOSTOT is under evaluation for potential deprecation and replacement with a mechanism to.
   c. When the EC domain is implemented in conjunction with the EX domain.
   d. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or findings general observation-class qualifiers.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/FA/assumptions.md -->
## FA

1. The Findings About domain shares all qualities and conventions of findings observations.
2. see § 6.4.1, When to Use Findings About Events or Interventions; and §8.6.3.
3. see § 6.4.2, Naming Findings About Domains, for advice on splitting the FA domain.
4. Some variables in the events and interventions domains (e.g., OCCUR, SEV.
   a. Associations between some findings about cardiovascular interventions or events and their.
5. When data collection establishes a relationship between FA records and an events or interventions record.
   a. The FAOBJ variable alone is not sufficient to establish a relationship.
6. Any Identifier variables, Timing variables, or Findings general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the FA domain.
**§6.4.1 When to Use Findings About Events or Interventions**
The Findings About Events or Interventions structure (or "FA structure") is intended, as its name implies.
Criterion 1: Data or observations that have different timing from an associated event or intervention as a whole
Per §6.2.1, Adverse Events, assumption 7.e, "It is the sponsor's responsibility to define an event." One common practice is to.
A finding that is about part of an event, rather than the event as a whole, meets this criterion for the use of FA.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/FT/assumptions.md -->
## FT

The following assumptions are unique to the FT domain:
1. A functional test is not a subjective assessment of how the subject generally performs a task.
2. Functional tests have documented methods for administration and analysis and require a subject to perform specific activities.
**QRS Shared Assumptions**
The following assumptions are common to the FT and QS domains as well as the Clinical Classifications use case of the RS domain.
1. The name of a QRS instrument is described under the variable --CAT in the relevant QRS domain (i.e., FT, QS, RS).
   a. The QRS Naming Rules for --CAT, --TEST, and --TESTCD and the list of QRS instruments that have.
   b. Refer to the following CDISC CT codelists for QRS instrument --CAT terminology:
   i. Category of Clinical Classification
      ii. Category of Functional Test
      iii. Category of Questionnaire
   c. QRS --TESTCD/--TEST terminology codelists are listed separately by instrument name.
2. Names of subcategories for groups of items/questions are described under the --SCAT variable.
   a. --SCAT values are not included in the CDISC CT system but rather controlled see the QRS.
3. There are cases where QRS CRFs do not include numeric "standardized responses" assigned to text responses.
4. Sponsors should always consult published QRS supplements for guidance on submitting derived information in a SDTMIG QRS domain.
   a. The following rules apply for "total"-type scores in QRS datasets.
   i. QRS subtotal, total, etc. scores listed on the CRF are considered captured data and are.
      ii. QRS subtotal, total, etc. scores not listed on the CRF but documented in an associated.
      iii. QRS subtotal, total, etc. scores not listed on the CRF.
   b. The QRS instrument subtotal or total score, which is the sum of the numeric responses for an.
   i. If operationally derived by the sponsor, it is the sponsor's responsibility to set the --DRVFL.
5. The variable --REPNUM variable is populated when there are multiple repeats of the same question.
6. The actual version number of an instrument is represented in the --CAT value as designated by the QRS Terminology Team.
   a. Notify the QRS Terminology Team that the instrument has a specific or multiple version numbers.
   b. Consider the use of the --GRPID variable to indicate the instrument's version number prior to.
   c. The sponsor is expected to provide information about the version used for each QRS instrument.
   d. The sponsor is expected to provide information about the scoring rules in the metadata.
7. If the variable --TEST is represented with verbatim text >40 characters.
   a. The instrument's annotated CRF can also be used as a reference for the full text in both of.
8. --EVAL and --EVALID must not be used to model QRS data in SDTM.
9. All standard QRS supplement development is coordinated with the CDISC SDS QRS Subteam as the governing body.
   a. Sponsors may participate and/or request the development of additional QRS supplements and.
   i. Once generated, the QRS supplement is posted on the CDISC website (cdisc.org).
      ii. Sponsors should always consult the published QRS supplements for guidance on submitting.
10. Any identifiers, timing variables, or findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to a QRS domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/GF/assumptions.md -->
## GF

**GF - Description/Overview**
A findings domain that contains data related to the structure, function, evolution, mapping.
This domain includes but is not limited to assessments and results for genetic variation and transcription.
The GF domain is used for findings from characteristics assessed from nucleic acids and may include subsequent inferences and/or.
**GF - Specification**
gf.xpt, Genomics Findings - Findings.
CDISC/NCI codelists will be available in CDISC CT, available at cancer.gov
**GF - Assumptions**
1. The Genomics Findings domain is used to represent findings related to the structure, function, evolution, mapping.
2. Regarding genetic testing on non-host organisms , the following additional assumptions apply:
   a. Tests that give genetic results on non-host organisms that have been identified in subject.
   b. If the purpose of the test is to detect or determine the identity of a viable.
   c. Tests that are used to determine the resistance/susceptibility of a non-host organism to a.
   d. If the test provides both genetic data and susceptibility/resistance data.
3. The platform used to detect the finding may be represented in SPDEVID.
4. Values populated in GFCAT and GFSCAT are sponsor-defined and there is no CDISC CT for these variables.
5. Genomic symbols are represented in GFSYM.
   a. GFTESTCD and GFTEST should not include genomic names or symbols.
   b. For human genetic data, standard nomenclature populated in variable GFSYM must be obtained.
6. When populating GFGENSR, caution should be exercised for annotations of loci where more than 1 annotation applies.
7. Values populated in GFGENREF, GFSEQID, and GFPVRID should reflect the level of granularity collected (e.g., version, build.
8. GFMETHOD lists wet lab techniques for the execution of genomics or genetic testing.
9. The following variables generally would not be used in GF: --POS, --BODSYS, --ORNRLO, ORNRHI, --STNRLO, --STNRHI, --STNRC.
**GF - Example 4 (p227)**
DNA extracted from tumor tissue is amplified and the resulting amplicons are resolved using wet laboratory methodology capillary.
Note the use of NHOID, which is populated with the name of the organism to which the testing applies.

## HO

1. The Healthcare Encounters (HO) dataset includes inpatient and outpatient healthcare events.
2. Values of HOTERM typically describe the location or place of the healthcare encounter.
3. Data collected about healthcare encounters may include the reason for the encounter.
   a. The supplemental qualifier with QNAM = "HOINDC" would be used to represent the.
   b. The supplemental qualifier with QNAM = "HOREAS" would be used to represent a reason for the.
4. If collected data includes the name of the provider or the facility where the encounter took place.
5. Any identifier variables, timing variables, or Events general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the HO domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/IE/assumptions.md -->
## IE

1. For the complete list of inclusion/exclusion criteria, see § 7.4.1, Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria.
2. This domain should be used to document the exceptions to inclusion or exclusion criteria at the time that eligibility for study.
3. IETEST is to be used only for the verbatim description of the inclusion or exclusion criteria.
4. The following qualifiers would generally not be used in IE: --MODIFY, --POS, --BODSYS, --ORRESU, --ORNRLO, --ORNRHI, --STRESN.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/IS/assumptions.md -->
## IS

**IS - Description/Overview**
A findings domain for assessments of antigen induced humoral or cell-mediated immune response in the subject.
is.xpt, Immunogenicity Specimen Assessments - Findings.
**IS - Assumptions**
1. The Immunogenicity Specimen Assessments (IS) domain holds assessments that describe whether a therapy (e.g., biologic, drug.
2. The IS domain also holds assessments that describe whether an allergen, microorganism.
3. Assessments about all other types of "induced" humoral (antibody) immune response in a subject will also be represented in the.
4. Flow cytometry data should be modeled in the Cell Phenotype Findings (CP) domain, §6.3.5.3.
5. Microbial antigen/antibody (Ag/Ab) combination tests should be represented in the Microbiology Specimen (MB) domain.
6. Measurements of cytokines, chemokines, and complement proteins should be represented in the Laboratory Test Results (LB) domain.
7. The IS domain variable ISBDAGNT (Binding Agent) is currently supported by 2 CT codelists: Microorganism (MICROORG) and Binding.
   a. For antidrug antibody (ADA) tests, the ISBDAGNT variable is used to represent the free-text.
   b. For mixed-allergens panel tests, submission values represented in the ISBDAGNT variable should.
8. The IS domain variable ISTSTOPO (Test Operational Objective) is supported by a nonextensible CT codelist containing the values.
9. For vaccine studies, in order to distinguish collected data between study vaccine-induced immunogenicity and immunogenicity.
   a. For immunological data pertaining to the study vaccine.
   b. For immunological data collected during the vaccine trial but which are not assessments about.
   c. For assessments measuring the induced-antibody response, ISSCAT = HUMORAL IMMUNITY.
   d. For assessments measuring the induced-cellular response, ISSCAT = CELLULAR IMMUNITY.
10. Any Identifier variables, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the IS domain.
**IS - Examples Notes**
**Example 1 (Tiered ADA testing)**
- Row 1: Shows the screening of the presence of ADA to drug AZ-007. - Row 2: Shows the confirmation of the previously detected.
**Examples 8-9 (Microneutralization and OPK)**
The next 2 examples show data from microneutralization and opsonophagocytic killing (OPK) assays.
**Example 10 (Sjögren's syndrome autoantibody tests)**
Sjögren's syndrome (SS) is a systemic autoimmune disease characterized by dry eyes and dry mouth.
Rows 2 and 3 are grouped together using ISGRPID="1a"; this means the titer result in row 2 is specifically related to the.
**Example 11 (Mixed animal allergens test)**
ISBDAGNT is used to house the generic but controlled value "ANIMAL MIX ANTIGENS.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/MB/assumptions.md -->
## MB

**6.3.5.7 Microbiology Domains**
The microbiology domains include Microbiology Specimen (MB) and Microbiology Susceptibility (MS).
**MB - Description/Overview**
A findings domain that represents non-host organisms identified including bacteria, viruses, parasites, protozoa and fungi.
**MB - Assumptions**
1. Representation of findings in the Microbiology Specimen domain should be handled as follows:
   a. In cases of tests that target an organism, group of organisms, or antigen for identification.
   i. MBTSTDTL should be "DETECTION".
      ii. The result should generally be "PRESENT"/"ABSENT", "POSITIVE"/"NEGATIVE", or "INDETERMINATE".
   b. For non-targeted identification of organisms , the value for MBTESTCD/MBTEST should be.
   c. Culture characteristics covers concepts such as growth/no growth.
   i. MBTESTCD/MBTEST should be the name of the organism or group of organisms being characterized.
      ii. MBTSTDTL should be the name of the characteristic being described (e.g., "COLONY COUNT".
      iii. MBGRPID should be used to group characteristic records with the identification record of the.
      iv. CDISC CT Rules for Microbiology (MB/MS) domains are available at cdisc.org
2. MBDTC represents the date the specimen was collected.
3. If the specimen was cultured, the start and end date of culture are represented in the Biospecimen Events (BE) domain in BESTDTC.
   a. Culture dates can be connected to the MB record via MBREFID and BEREFID.
   b. If the same sample is associated with many biospecimen events and tests.
4. The variable NHOID is not allowed for use in the MB domain.

An events domain that contains data that includes the subject's prior medical history at the start of the trial.

1. Prior treatments, including prior medications and procedures, should be submitted in an appropriate dataset from the.
2. MH description and coding
   a. MHTERM is topic var and captures the verbatim term collected for the condition or event or the.

3. Additional categorization and grouping
   a. MHCAT and MHSCAT may be populated with the sponsor's predefined categorization of medical.
   i. This categorization should not group all records (within the MH domain) into one generic group.
      ii. Examples of MHCAT could include "General Medical History".
   b. MHGRPID may be used to link (or associate) different records together to form a block of.
4. Prespecified terms; presence or absence of events
   a. MHPRESP and MHOCCUR are used together to indicate whether the condition in MHTERM was.
   b. MHOCCUR is used to indicate whether a prespecified medical condition occurred; a value of "Y".
   c. If a medical history event was reported using free text.
   d. MHSTAT and MHREASND provide information about prespecified medical history questions for which.
   e. When medical history events are collected with the recording of free text.
5. Timing variables
   a. MHENRF may be used when this relative timing assessment is coincident with the start of the.
   b. Additional timing variables (e.g., MHSTRF) may be used when appropriate.
6. MH event date type
   a. MHEVDTYP is a domain-specific variable that can be used to indicate the aspect of the event.
   b. In such a case, it is recommended that the 2 records be linked by means such as a common value.
7. Any identifiers, timing variables, or Events general observation-class qualifiers may be added to the MH domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/MS/assumptions.md -->
## MS

**MS - Description/Overview**
A findings domain that represents drug susceptibility testing results only.
**MS - Specification**
ms.xpt, Microbiology Susceptibility - Findings.
1In this column, an asterisk (*) indicates that the var may be CT.
**MS - Assumptions**
1. Microbiology Susceptibility testing includes testing of the following types:
   a. Phenotypic drug susceptibility testing (qualitative).
   i. For studies using qualitative testing methods, MSAGENT, MSCONC.
      ii. For studies using quantitative testing methods, MSAGENT is used to represent the drug being.
      iii. As in 1.a.ii, MSAGENT populated with the drug whose action would be affected by the genetic.
   b. Genotypic tests that provide results in terms of specific changes to nucleotides, codons.
   c. CDISC CT Rules for Microbiology (MB/MS) domains are available at cdisc.org
2. MSDTC represents the date the specimen was collected.
3. If the specimen was cultured, the start and end date of culture are represented in the Biospecimen Events (BE) domain in BESTDTC.
   a. Culture dates can be connected to the MS record via MSREFID and BEREFID.
   b. If the same sample is associated with many biospecimen events and tests.
4. NHOID is a sponsor-defined, intuitive name of the non-host organism being tested.
5. Any Identifiers, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the MS domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/RELREC/assumptions.md -->
## RELREC

The Related Records (RELREC) special-purpose dataset is used to describe relationships between records for a subject (relating.
A relationship is defined by adding a record to RELREC for each record to be related and by assigning a unique character.
**Relating Peer Records**
Records expressing a relationship are specified using the key variables STUDYID, RDOMAIN , and USUBJID.
The RELREC dataset should be used to represent either: - explicit relationships.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/TA/assumptions.md -->
## TA

**Trial Design Model Overview (§7.1)**
**§7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model**
ICH E3, Guidance for Industry, Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports (available at (url)), §9.1, calls for a brief.
Each Annex corresponds to an example trial, and each shows a diagram describing the study design and a table showing the schedule.
The Trial Design Model provides a standardized way to describe those aspects of the planned conduct of a clinical trial shown in.
The standard Trial Design Datasets will allow reviewers to: - Clearly and quickly grasp the design of a clinical trial - Compare.
Modeling a clinical trial in this standardized way requires the explicit statement of certain decision rules that may not be.
Prospective modeling of the design of a clinical trial should lead to a clearer, better protocol.
Retrospective modeling of the design of a clinical trial should ensure a clear description of how the trial protocol was.
**§7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts**
A clinical trial is a scientific experiment involving human subjects, intended to address certain scientific questions (i.e.
See the CDISC Glossary (cdisc.org) for more complete definitions of clinical trial and objective.
**§7.1.3 Current and Future Contents of the Trial Design Model**
Datasets currently in the Trial Design Model include: - Trial Arms: Describes the sequences of elements in each epoch for each.
The Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria (TI) dataset is discussed in §7.4.1, Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria.
The Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met (IE) domain § 6.3.4, Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met.
The current Trial Design Model has limitations in representing protocols.
The last 2 situations arise in dose-escalation studies where increasing doses are given until stopping criteria are met.
Some dose-escalation studies enroll a new cohort of subjects for each new dose, and so, at the planning stage.
Other dose-escalation studies give new doses to a continuing group of subjects.
There may also be limitations in representing other patterns of Elements within a Study Cell that are more complex than a simple.
For the purpose of submissions about trials that have already completed, these limitations are not critical.
**Trial Arms and Trial Elements (§7.2)**
**§7.2.1 Trial Arms (TA) - Description/Overview**
A trial design domain that contains each planned arm in the trial.
This section contains: - The Trial Arms dataset and assumptions - A series of example trials.
**§7.2.1 Trial Arms (TA) - Example 2**
The following diagram for a crossover trial does not use the crossing slanted lines sometimes used to represent crossover trials.
The next diagram for this crossover trial shows the prospective view of the trial; it identifies the epoch and arms of the trial.
The next diagram shows the retrospective view of the trial.
The last diagram for this trial shows the trial from the viewpoint of blinded participants.
The following table illustrates the trial design matrix for this crossover example trial.
It is straightforward to produce the TA dataset for this crossover trial from the diagram showing arms and epochs.
**§7.2.1.1 Trial Arms Issues - Distinguishing Between Branches and Transitions**
Both the Branch and Transition columns contain rules, but the 2 columns represent 2 different types of rules.
Branch rules represent forks in the trial flowchart, giving rise to separate arms.
The rule underlying a branch in the trial design appears in multiple records, once for each "fork" of the branch.
Within any one record, there is no choice (no "if" clause) in the value of the branch condition.
Transition rules are used for choices within an arm.
The value for TATRANS does contain a choice (an "if" clause).
In Example Trial 4, subjects who receive 1, 2, 3, or 4 cycles of treatment A are all considered to belong to arm A.
In modeling a trial, decisions may have to be made about whether a decision point in the flow chart represents the separation of.
This decision will depend on the comparisons of interest in the trial.
Some trials refer to groups of subjects who follow a particular path through the trial as "cohorts," particularly if the groups.
The term "cohort" is used with different meanings in different protocols and does not always correspond to an arm.
**§7.2.2.1 Trial Elements Issues - Distinguishing Elements, Study Cells, and Epochs**
It is easy to confuse elements, which are reusable trial building blocks, with study cells and with epochs.
In part, this is because many trials have epochs for which the same element appears in all arms.
It also is natural to use the same name (e.g., screen, follow-up) for both such an epoch and the single element that appears.
Confusion can also arise from the fact that in the blinded treatment portions of blinded trials.
In describing a trial, one way to avoid confusion between elements and epochs is to include "Element" or "Epoch" in the values of.
It becomes tedious to do this in every case, but can be useful to resolve confusion when it arises or is likely to arise.
The difference between epoch and element is perhaps clearest in crossover trials.
In TA Example Trial 2, as for most crossover trials, the analysis of pharmacokinetic (PK) results would include both treatment.
"Treatment effect" derives from element (placebo, 5 mg, 10 mg), whereas "period effect" derives from the epoch (first, second.
**§7.2.2.1 Trial Elements Issues - Transitions Between Elements**
The transition between one element and the next can be thought of as a 3-step process:
The subject remains in the current element until step 3, at which point the subject transitions to the new element.
There are no gaps between elements.
As illustrated in the table, executing a transition depends on information that is split between the TE and the TA datasets.
It can be useful, in the process of working out the Trial Design (TD) datasets.
The following table shows a fragment of such a table for TA Example Trial 4.
The activity that marks a subject's entry into the fourth element in arm A is "First dose of treatment Element.
This is not the subject's very first dose of treatment A, but it is their first dose in this element.
**TA-Specific Assumptions**
The TA and TE datasets are interrelated, and they provide the building blocks for the development of subject-level treatment.
1. TAETORD is an integer.
2. Elements in different arms with the same value of TAETORD may or may not be at the same time.
3. TABRANCH describes the outcome of a branch decision point in the trial design for subjects in the arm.
4. Branch decision points may be based on decision processes other than randomizations.
5. There is usually some gap in time between the performance of a randomization and the start of randomized treatment.
6. The sequence of elements represented in the trial arms should be the complete, longest path.
7. If an element does not end with a decision that could lead to a shortened path within the arm, then TATRANS will be blank.
8. EPOCH is not strictly necessary for describing the sequence of elements in an arm path.
9. EPOCH should be assigned in such a way that elements from different arms with the same value of EPOCH are "comparable" in some.
10. EPOCH values for multiple similar epochs:
   a. When a study design includes multiple epochs with the same purpose (e.g.
11. A set of records with a common value of both ARMCD and EPOCH constitute the description of a study cell.
12. EPOCH may be used as a timing variable in other datasets, such as Exposure (EX) and Disposition (DS).

7. Specifying TESTRL for an element that serves the first element of an arm in the TA dataset involves defining the start of the.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/TI/assumptions.md -->
## TI

**§7.4 Trial Eligibility and Summary (TI, TS) - Introduction**
This section contains the Trial Design (TD) datasets that describe:
- Subject eligibility criteria for trial participation (§7.4.1, Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria (TI)) - The characteristics of.
The TI and TS datasets are tabular synopses of parts of the study protocol.
**TI - Assumptions**
The variable TIRL was included in the Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria (TI) domain in anticipation of developing a way to.
The IE domain (§ 6.3.4, Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met) contains records only for inclusion and exclusion criteria that.
1. If inclusion/exclusion criteria were amended during the trial, then each complete set of criteria must be included in the TI.
2. Protocol version numbers should be used to identify criteria versions.
3. Individual criteria do not have versions.
4. IETEST contains the text of the inclusion/exclusion criterion.
5. If a criterion text is <200 characters, it goes in IETEST; if the text is >200 characters.
**§7.5 How to Model the Design of a Clinical Trial**
The following steps allow the modeler to move from more-familiar concepts, such as arms, to less-familiar concepts.
The actual process of modeling a trial may depart from these numbered steps.
Some steps will overlap; there may be several iterations; and not all steps are relevant for all studies.
1. Start from the flow chart or schema diagram usually included in the trial protocol.
2. On whether the subject responded to treatment?
3. If the trial has multiple branching points, check whether all the branches that have been identified really lead to different.
4. For each arm, identify the major time periods of treatment and non-treatment a subject assigned to that arm will go through.
5. Define the starting point of each element.
6. Re-examine the sequences of elements that make up the various arms and consider alternative element definitions.
7. Compare the various arms.
8. Identify the visits planned for the trial.
9. For oncology trials or other trials with disease assessments that are not necessarily tied to visits.
10. If the protocol includes data collection that is triggered by the occurrence of certain events, interventions, or findings.
11. If inclusion and exclusion criteria were amended so that subjects entered under different versions.
12. Populate the TS dataset with summary information.

**Use of Null Flavor**
The variable TSVALNF is based on the idea of a "null flavor" as embodied in the ISO 21090 standard.
The proposal to include a null flavor variable to supplement the TSVAL variable in the Trial Summary Information (TS) dataset.
The SDS Team also decided to specify the use of a null-flavor variable in the TS domain with SDTMIG v3.4 as a way of testing the.
The CT for null flavor, which supersedes Appendix C1, Supplemental Qualifiers Name Codes, is included below.
NullFlavor Enumeration (OID: 2.16.840.1.113883.5.1008)
WARNING - Use of this null flavor does provide information that may be a breach of confidentiality.
The numbers in column 1 of the table describe the hierarchy of these values:
- No information - Invalid - Other - Positive infinity - Negative infinity - Unencoded - Derived - Unknown - Asked but unknown -.
It merely confirms that the primary piece of data is null.
The values at level 2 provide a little more information, distinguishing between situations where the primary piece of data is not.
The null flavor PINF provides the most complete information possible in this case.

## TU

**6.3.12 Tumor/Lesion Domains**
**TU - Description/Overview**
A findings domain that represents data that uniquely identifies tumors, lesions, or locations of interest under study.
The TU domain represents data that uniquely identifies tumors, lesions, or locations of interest.
- a unique tumor ID value - anatomical location of the tumor - method used to identify the tumor - role of the individual.
1. The TU domain should contain only 1 record for each unique tumor/lesion/location of interest identified by an assessor (e.g.
   a. A new tumor/lesion may emerge at any time during a study; therefore.
   b. If a tumor/lesion identified at baseline subsequently splits into separate distinct.
   c. In situations where a re-baseline of targets and non-targets is required (e.g.
2. TRLNKID is used to relate an identification record in the TU domain to assessment records in the Tumor/Lesion Results (TR) domain.
3. For some response criteria (e.g., Lugano, Kumar IMWG 2016), tumors are assessed by location of interest.
4. During the course of a trial, when a new tumor/lesion is identified.
5. The additional anatomical location variables --LAT, --DIR, --PORTOT were added from the SDTM.
6. In the oncology setting, when a new tumor is identified, a record must be included in both the TU and TR domains.
   a. The occurrence of a new tumor/lesion is the sole piece of information that a sponsor collects.
   b. The occurrence of a new tumor/lesion and the anatomical location of that newly identified.
   c. The sponsor records the occurrence of a new tumor/lesion to the same level of detail as target.
7. The acceptance flag variable (TUACPTFL) identifies records that have been determined to be the accepted assessments/measurements.
8. The evaluator-specified variable TUEVALID is used in conjunction with TUEVAL to provide additional detail regarding who is.
9. If indicator questions for specific types of tumor or lesions are collected , then these TUTESTs will be included in TU.
10. The TUTEST Disease Recurrence Relative Location is used identify the region or relative location for the disease recurrence.
11. The following proposed supplemental qualifiers would be used for oncology studies to represent information regarding previous.
12. When additional data are collected about a procedure used for tumor/lesion identification.
13. Any Identifiers, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the TU domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/TV/assumptions.md -->
## TV

**§7.3 Schedule for Assessments (TV, TD, TM) - Introduction**
This section contains the Trial Design (TD) datasets that describe:
- The protocol-defined planned schedule of subject encounters at the healthcare facility where the study is being conducted.
The Trial Visits (TV) and TD datasets provide the planned scheduling of assessments to which a subject's actual visits and.
**TV - Description/Overview**
Visits are defined as "clinical encounters" and are described using the timing variables VISIT, VISITNUM, and VISITDY.
**TV - Assumptions**
1. Although the general structure of the Trial Visits (TV) dataset is "One Record per Planned Visit per Arm," for many clinical.
2. A visit may start in one element and end in another.
3. TVSTRL describes the scheduling of the visit and should reflect the wording in the protocol.
4. In the first case, if visit 3 were delayed for some reason, visit 4 would be unaffected.
   a. Case 1: Visit 3 starts 2 weeks after RFSTDTC.
   b. Case 2: Visit 3 starts 2 weeks after RFSTDTC.
5. In such a case, TVENRL may be left blank to indicate that the visit ends on the same day it starts.
6. The values of VISITNUM in the TV dataset are the valid values of VISITNUM for planned visits.

## UR

1. Any Identifiers, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the UR domain.

<!-- source: knowledge_base/domains/VS/assumptions.md -->
## VS

1. In cases where the LOINC dictionary is used for vital sign tests, the Perm var VSLOINC may be used.
2. If a reference range is available for a vital signs test, the variables VSORNRLO, VSORNRHI.
3. Associations between some vital sign tests and qualifier codelists are described in the VS codetable, available at cdisc.org
4. Any Identifiers, Timing variables, or Findings general observation class qualifiers may be added to the VS domain.
